frontmatter.name: GreekMenuPlanner
frontmatter.description: Plans complete Greek menus for special occasions and everyday dining

agent-menu.mainInstruction:
# Greek Menu Planner

You are an expert in Greek hospitality and cuisine, skilled at creating memorable dining experiences that honor Greek culinary traditions.

## Your Approach
- Design balanced menus with proper progression of flavors
- Consider seasonal availability of ingredients
- Incorporate regional specialties from across Greece
- Account for dietary restrictions while maintaining authenticity
- Include wine and beverage pairings when appropriate

## Output Format
Present menus with courses clearly organized, including meze, main courses, and desserts. Provide a cooking timeline and preparation tips.

slashActions.name: Plan Menu
slashActions.description: Create a complete Greek menu for any occasion

frontmatter.name: GreekRecipeChef
frontmatter.description: Creates authentic Greek recipes with traditional techniques and regional variations

agent-recipe.mainInstruction:
# Greek Recipe Chef

You are an expert Greek chef specializing in traditional and contemporary Greek cuisine. Your knowledge spans from ancient recipes to modern Greek gastronomy.

## Your Approach
- Honor traditional Greek cooking methods while adapting for modern kitchens
- Emphasize fresh, seasonal Mediterranean ingredients
- Share cultural context and regional origins of dishes
- Provide clear, step-by-step instructions suitable for any skill level
- Suggest authentic ingredient substitutions when needed

## Output Format
Structure recipes with clear sections including ingredients, preparation time, cooking method, and serving suggestions. Include tips for achieving authentic flavors.

slashActions.name: Create Recipe
slashActions.description: Generate a complete Greek recipe with traditional techniques

flow_menu_plan.description:
# Plan Greek Menu

This flow guides you through creating a complete Greek menu for any occasion.

1. [Define the Occasion](1.Define%20the%20Occasion.md)
2. [Set Guest Parameters](2.Set%20Guest%20Parameters.md)
3. [Select Meze Course](3.Select%20Meze%20Course.md)
4. [Choose Main Dishes](4.Choose%20Main%20Dishes.md)
5. [Add Accompaniments](5.Add%20Accompaniments.md)
6. [Plan Desserts](6.Plan%20Desserts.md)
7. [Create Cooking Timeline](7.Create%20Cooking%20Timeline.md)

tasks.name: Define the Occasion
tasks.instructions:
# Define the Occasion

The occasion shapes every menu decision in Greek cooking:

## Everyday Dining
- Simple, nourishing meals
- Focus on seasonal ingredients
- One main dish with bread and salad
- Quick preparation time

## Sunday Family Lunch
- The most important meal of the week
- Multiple courses served simultaneously
- Roasted meats or traditional stews
- Extended leisurely dining

## Name Day / Birthday
- Celebratory dishes
- Generous portions for guests
- Traditional sweets required
- Alcohol accompaniments

## Easter (Πάσχα)
- Most important holiday menu
- Lamb is essential
- Specific traditional dishes
- Weekend-long preparation

## Christmas / New Year
- Roast pork or turkey
- Melomakarona, kourabiedes required
- Vasilopita for New Year
- Abundant meze selection

## Summer Gathering
- Light, fresh dishes
- Grilled seafood and meats
- Cold meze and salads
- Fruit-based desserts

tasks.name: Set Guest Parameters
tasks.instructions:
# Set Guest Parameters

Plan around your guests' needs:

## Number of Guests
- **2-4 people**: Intimate, fewer dishes needed
- **6-10 people**: Standard Greek gathering
- **12+ people**: Large celebration, buffet-style

## Dietary Considerations

### Religious Fasting (Νηστεία)
Many Greeks fast before major holidays:
- No meat, dairy, or eggs
- Seafood usually permitted
- Olive oil always allowed

### Vegetarian Guests
Greek cuisine is naturally vegetarian-friendly:
- Rich tradition of ladera dishes
- Substantial cheese and egg courses
- Beans and legumes as protein

### Allergies/Restrictions
- **Nut-free**: Modify baklava, skip walnut dishes
- **Gluten-free**: Many naturally GF options
- **Dairy-free**: Focus on ladera, seafood

## Cooking Capacity
- How many burners/oven space?
- Can you prep ahead?
- Do you need help?

## Budget
- Seafood and lamb are premium
- Legume dishes are economical
- Balance across courses

tasks.name: Select Meze Course
tasks.instructions:
# Select Meze Course

Meze is essential — Greeks never serve main courses without appetizers first.

## Cold Meze (prepare ahead)
- **Tzatziki** — yogurt, cucumber, garlic
- **Taramosalata** — fish roe dip (pink)
- **Melitzanosalata** — smoky eggplant dip
- **Fava** — yellow split pea purée (Santorini)
- **Htipiti** — spicy feta dip
- **Dolmades** — stuffed grape leaves

## Hot Meze
- **Saganaki** — fried cheese (pan or shrimp version)
- **Kolokithokeftedes** — zucchini fritters
- **Spanakopita** — spinach pie (individual portions)
- **Keftedakia** — mini meatballs
- **Grilled halloumi** — with lemon

## Always Include
- **Feta** — simple block with oregano and olive oil
- **Olives** — Kalamata or throumbes
- **Bread** — crusty loaf for dipping

## Quantity Guide
- 2-3 cold meze per gathering
- 1-2 hot meze
- Plan for people to fill up on meze (they will)

## Timing
Serve meze immediately when guests arrive with ouzo, wine, or tsipouro.

tasks.name: Choose Main Dishes
tasks.instructions:
# Choose Main Dishes

Select main courses based on occasion and season:

## Meat Mains

### Lamb (Αρνί)
- **Kleftiko** — slow-roasted in parchment
- **Lamb yiouvetsi** — with orzo pasta
- **Lamb chops** — grilled with lemon-oregano
- **Whole roasted lamb** — Easter tradition

### Pork (Χοιρινό)
- **Tigania** — pan-fried with wine
- **Pork souvlaki** — skewered and grilled
- **Roast pork** — with potatoes

### Chicken (Κοτόπουλο)
- **Lemon chicken** — with potatoes
- **Chicken stifado** — with onions
- **Kotopoulo kokkinisto** — in tomato sauce

## Seafood Mains
- **Grilled whole fish** — lavraki, tsipoura
- **Octopus** — grilled or stewed with wine
- **Shrimp saganaki** — in tomato-feta sauce
- **Bakaliaros** — fried cod with skordalia

## Vegetarian Mains
- **Moussaka** (can make vegetarian)
- **Yemista** — stuffed tomatoes and peppers
- **Briam** — roasted vegetable medley
- **Gigantes plaki** — giant beans in tomato

## Balance Tips
- One protein-heavy main
- One vegetable or grain-based option
- Consider offering both meat and seafood for larger groups

tasks.name: Add Accompaniments
tasks.instructions:
# Add Accompaniments

No Greek meal is complete without these accompaniments:

## Salads

### Greek Salad (Χωριάτικη)
The essential accompaniment:
- Tomato, cucumber, onion, green pepper
- Block of feta on top
- Kalamata olives, oregano
- Olive oil and red wine vinegar
- NO LETTUCE (unless you want mockery)

### Other Salads
- **Maroulosalata** — lettuce with dill and spring onion
- **Cabbage salad** — shredded, with lemon
- **Beet salad** — with garlic and vinegar
- **Politiki** — cabbage, carrot, peppers

## Starches

### Potatoes
- Lemon potatoes (oven-roasted)
- Fried potatoes
- Mashed potatoes (less common but loved)

### Rice/Pasta
- Pilafi — rice pilaf
- Orzo (kritharaki) — often baked with meat
- Hilopites — egg noodles

## Bread
- **Crusty village bread** — always present
- **Pita bread** — for souvlaki occasions
- **Lagana** — for Clean Monday

## Order More Than You Think
Greeks always have leftovers — it's expected and welcomed.

tasks.name: Plan Desserts
tasks.instructions:
# Plan Desserts

Greek desserts are rich and meant to be shared:

## Syrup-Soaked Sweets (Σιροπιαστά)
- **Baklava** — phyllo, nuts, honey syrup
- **Galaktoboureko** — custard in phyllo
- **Ravani/Revani** — semolina cake
- **Portokalopita** — orange phyllo cake
- **Kataifi** — shredded phyllo with nuts

## Cookies (Κουλουράκια)
- **Kourabiedes** — butter cookies with powdered sugar
- **Melomakarona** — honey-walnut cookies
- **Koulourakia** — twisted butter cookies
- **Paximadia** — twice-baked biscotti

## Spoon Sweets (Γλυκά του Κουταλιού)
Traditional preserved fruits served with coffee:
- Cherry, quince, fig, grape, orange
- Served in small portions with water

## Fresh Options
- **Greek yogurt** with honey and walnuts
- **Fresh fruit** — watermelon, figs, grapes
- **Karydopita** — walnut cake (less sweet)

## Holiday-Specific
- **Vasilopita** — New Year's cake with coin
- **Tsoureki** — Easter sweet bread
- **Diples** — fried honey pastry

## Serving Tips
- Serve with Greek coffee or espresso
- Desserts come AFTER clearing main course
- Smaller portions — these are rich
- Always offer fruit as lighter option

tasks.name: Create Cooking Timeline
tasks.instructions:
# Create Cooking Timeline

Plan backwards from serving time to ensure everything is ready:

## Day Before
- [ ] Shop for all ingredients
- [ ] Make dips (tzatziki, melitzanosalata) — flavors improve overnight
- [ ] Prepare dolmades
- [ ] Marinate meat
- [ ] Bake desserts (baklava, galaktoboureko)
- [ ] Make syrup for desserts

## Morning Of
- [ ] Prep all vegetables (wash, chop)
- [ ] Bring meat to room temperature
- [ ] Start any slow-cooking dishes (stews, roasts)
- [ ] Prepare stuffed vegetables
- [ ] Set the table

## 2 Hours Before
- [ ] Start oven dishes (potatoes, yemista)
- [ ] Prepare salad ingredients (don't dress yet)
- [ ] Slice bread
- [ ] Arrange cold meze on platters

## 1 Hour Before
- [ ] Start rice/pasta
- [ ] Cook hot meze
- [ ] Grill meats
- [ ] Warm dips if needed

## 30 Minutes Before
- [ ] Dress salads
- [ ] Final seasoning on dishes
- [ ] Plate and arrange
- [ ] Pour drinks for arrival

## Key Principles
- Don't stress about timing — Greek time is flexible
- Better to have food waiting than guests waiting
- Let guests snack on meze while you finish
- Accept help in the kitchen — it's social

flow_recipe_create.description:
# Create Greek Recipe

This flow guides you through creating an authentic Greek recipe with proper techniques and cultural context.

1. [Select Dish Category](1.Select%20Dish%20Category.md)
2. [Choose Region and Style](2.Choose%20Region%20and%20Style.md)
3. [Gather Ingredients](3.Gather%20Ingredients.md)
4. [Prepare Mise en Place](4.Prepare%20Mise%20en%20Place.md)
5. [Cook with Traditional Techniques](5.Cook%20with%20Traditional%20Techniques.md)
6. [Season and Adjust](6.Season%20and%20Adjust.md)
7. [Plate and Serve](7.Plate%20and%20Serve.md)

tasks.name: Select Dish Category
tasks.instructions:
# Select Dish Category

Identify the type of dish you want to create. Greek cuisine offers diverse categories:

- **Meze (Μεζέδες)**: Small plates for sharing - dips, cheese, olives
- **Soups (Σούπες)**: Avgolemono, fasolada, trachanas
- **Main Courses (Κύρια Πιάτα)**: Moussaka, pastitsio, grilled meats
- **Seafood (Θαλασσινά)**: Fresh fish, octopus, shrimp saganaki
- **Vegetarian (Λαδερά)**: Olive oil-based vegetable dishes
- **Pies (Πίτες)**: Spanakopita, tiropita, regional variations
- **Desserts (Γλυκά)**: Baklava, galaktoboureko, kourabiedes

Consider the occasion and number of servings needed.

tasks.name: Choose Region and Style
tasks.instructions:
# Choose Region and Style

Greek cuisine varies significantly by region. Select the style that fits your dish:

## Mainland Regions
- **Athens/Attica**: Urban fusion, diverse influences
- **Thessaloniki**: Ottoman influences, spicier flavors
- **Peloponnese**: Rustic dishes, olive oil-centric
- **Epirus**: Mountain cuisine, pies, dairy-rich

## Island Regions  
- **Crete**: Dakos, wild greens, distinct olive oil
- **Cyclades**: Simple, fresh seafood, local cheeses
- **Dodecanese**: Eastern Mediterranean influences
- **Ionian Islands**: Italian influences, sofrito, pastitsada

## Traditional vs Modern
- **Παραδοσιακό (Traditional)**: Authentic grandmother recipes
- **Σύγχρονο (Modern)**: Contemporary interpretations

tasks.name: Gather Ingredients
tasks.instructions:
# Gather Ingredients

Assemble all ingredients, prioritizing authentic Greek products:

## Essential Greek Pantry
- **Olive Oil**: Extra virgin Greek olive oil (koroneiki variety)
- **Feta**: P.D.O. Greek feta from sheep/goat milk
- **Herbs**: Fresh oregano (rigani), dill, mint, parsley
- **Spices**: Cinnamon, allspice, bay leaves, mastiha

## Fresh Ingredients
- **Produce**: Tomatoes, eggplant, zucchini, lemons
- **Aromatics**: Garlic, onions, spring onions
- **Proteins**: Quality meat, fresh fish, eggs

## Substitution Notes
- If Greek feta unavailable, use Bulgarian feta (not French)
- Fresh oregano can be replaced with dried Greek oregano
- Mastiha can be omitted or replaced with a drop of anise

Weigh and measure everything before starting.

tasks.name: Prepare Mise en Place
tasks.instructions:
# Prepare Mise en Place

Proper preparation is essential for Greek cooking success:

## Cutting Techniques
- **Dice onions fine** for sauces, larger for stews
- **Slice eggplant** consistently for even cooking
- **Mince garlic** at the last minute to preserve flavor
- **Chop herbs roughly** — Greeks prefer rustic cuts

## Pre-cooking Steps
- Salt eggplant slices and let drain (30 minutes)
- Soak dried beans overnight
- Bring meat to room temperature
- Drain and crumble feta as needed

## Organize Your Station
- Group ingredients by cooking stage
- Pre-measure olive oil (you'll use plenty)
- Prepare your cooking vessels
- Have serving dishes ready

## Equipment Check
- Heavy-bottomed pans for even heat
- Clay pots (γιουβέτσι) for traditional cooking
- Phyllo brush for pies
- Mortar and pestle for skordalia

tasks.name: Cook with Traditional Techniques
tasks.instructions:
# Cook with Traditional Techniques

Apply authentic Greek cooking methods:

## Foundational Techniques

### Yiahni (Γιαχνί)
Slow-braised dishes in tomato sauce with olive oil. The base of many Greek stews.

### Ladero (Λαδερό)
Vegetables cooked generously in olive oil until meltingly tender. Not stir-fried — slow-cooked.

### Avgolemono (Αυγολέμονο)
Egg-lemon sauce: temper eggs with hot liquid, never boil after adding.

### Sto Fourno (Στο Φούρνο)
Oven-roasted with lemon, oregano, and olive oil. Low and slow.

## Heat Management
- Start aromatics in cold olive oil
- Build layers of flavor — don't rush the sofrito
- Add wine and let it evaporate
- Tomatoes go in after onions are soft

## The Greek "Secret"
- More olive oil than you think
- Fresh lemon at the end
- Let dishes rest before serving

tasks.name: Season and Adjust
tasks.instructions:
# Season and Adjust

Fine-tune your dish with proper Greek seasoning:

## Tasting Protocol
- Taste throughout cooking, not just at the end
- Check salt levels after adding feta (it's salty)
- Balance acidity with sweetness if needed

## Essential Seasonings

### Salt
- Season in layers during cooking
- Coarse sea salt for finishing
- Remember: olives and feta add salt

### Acid
- Fresh lemon juice is essential
- Add at the very end for brightness
- Red wine vinegar for specific dishes

### Herbs (Fresh vs Dried)
- **Fresh**: Dill, mint, parsley — add at end
- **Dried**: Oregano — can cook with dish
- **Bay leaves**: Remove before serving

## Final Adjustments
- Drizzle with fresh olive oil
- Add cracked pepper
- Taste and adjust salt/lemon balance
- Let flavors meld for a few minutes

tasks.name: Plate and Serve
tasks.instructions:
# Plate and Serve

Present your dish in the Greek tradition:

## Serving Style
Greeks serve family-style from shared platters. Individual plating is more modern.

## Traditional Presentation
- Use simple white or terracotta dishes
- Generous drizzle of olive oil on top
- Fresh herbs as garnish (not decorative — to be eaten)
- Lemon wedges on the side
- Crusty bread always accompanies the meal

## Temperature
- Many Greek dishes are served at room temperature
- Ladera are best not piping hot
- Grilled items should be served immediately
- Dips at cool room temperature

## Accompaniments
- Greek salad (no lettuce — tomato, cucumber, onion, feta)
- Tzatziki or yogurt
- Kalamata olives
- Fresh bread for sopping up sauces

## The Greek Table
- Serve everything at once (except dessert)
- Food goes in the center
- Encourage sharing and conversation
- No rushing — meals are for enjoying

frontmatter.title: Modern Greek
frontmatter.description: Contemporary interpretations of Greek cuisine

children.content:
# Modern Greek Cuisine (Σύγχρονη)

Contemporary Greek cooking that honors tradition while embracing innovation and global techniques.

## Characteristics
- Traditional flavors, modern presentation
- Lighter preparations
- International techniques applied to Greek ingredients
- Restaurant-style plating
- Fusion elements thoughtfully incorporated

## Modern Approaches
- Deconstructed classics (moussaka reimagined)
- Smaller, refined portions
- Temperature contrasts
- Texture play
- Local ingredients showcased creatively

## Notable Influences
- Athens fine dining scene
- Greek diaspora chefs returning home
- Farm-to-table movement
- Molecular gastronomy (subtle)

## Balance Principle
Always maintain connection to Greek flavor profiles:
- Olive oil remains central
- Lemon brightness
- Fresh herbs
- Feta and yogurt
- Mediterranean vegetables

frontmatter.title: Traditional Era
frontmatter.description: Classic grandmother recipes passed down through generations

children.content:
# Traditional Greek Cooking (Παραδοσιακή)

Authentic recipes as prepared for generations by Greek grandmothers (yiayiades).

## Characteristics
- Time-honored techniques
- No shortcuts or modern conveniences
- Seasonal and local ingredients
- Recipe by taste, not measurement
- Stories and traditions attached

## Cooking Style
- Long, slow cooking times
- Wood-fired ovens when possible
- Handmade phyllo dough
- Fresh herbs from the garden
- Preserved foods for winter

## Learning Approach
- Respect the original recipe first
- Understand why each step exists
- Taste constantly as yiayia did
- Accept variation between families

## Common Phrases
- "Στο μάτι" (by eye) — measuring by intuition
- "Μέχρι να ροδίσει" — until it turns golden
- "Σιγά-σιγά" — slowly, slowly

config.label: Cooking Era
config.description: Traditional grandmother recipes or modern Greek cuisine

frontmatter.title: Fasting (Nistisimo)
frontmatter.description: Vegan Greek cooking for Orthodox fasting periods

children.content:
# Nistisimo (Νηστίσιμο) - Greek Fasting

Greek Orthodox fasting cuisine excludes meat, dairy, eggs, and sometimes oil and wine.

## Fasting Levels
- **Regular fasting**: No meat, dairy, eggs
- **Strict fasting**: Also no olive oil or wine
- **Shellfish allowed**: On most fasting days

## Traditional Fasting Dishes
- Fasolada (bean soup)
- Fakes (lentil soup)
- Revithia (chickpea stew)
- Dolmades with rice (no meat)
- Halvas semolina (no butter/eggs)
- Taramosalata (fish roe is permitted)

## Major Fasting Periods
- Great Lent (40 days before Easter)
- Advent (before Christmas)
- Dormition Fast (August)
- Apostles' Fast (after Pentecost)

## Tips
- These dishes are delicious year-round
- Focus on umami from legumes and tomatoes
- Tahini replaces dairy for creaminess

frontmatter.title: Seafood Focus
frontmatter.description: Mediterranean seafood-centric Greek cooking

children.content:
# Seafood-Focused Greek Cooking

Island and coastal Greek cuisine centering on fresh fish and seafood.

## Key Principles
- Freshness is paramount
- Simple preparations highlight quality
- Olive oil and lemon are essential
- Don't overcook fish

## Classic Seafood Dishes
- Grilled whole fish (lavraki, tsipoura)
- Octopus (grilled, stewed, with pasta)
- Calamari (fried or stuffed)
- Shrimp saganaki
- Kakavia (fisherman's soup)
- Marinated anchovies
- Mussels with ouzo

## Cooking Methods
- **Grilling**: Whole fish with olive oil and lemon
- **Plaki**: Baked in tomato sauce with vegetables
- **Saganaki**: Pan-cooked in tomato-feta sauce
- **Psito**: Oven-roasted with potatoes

## Sourcing Tips
- Whole fish over fillets
- Wild-caught when possible
- Ask your fishmonger what's fresh today

frontmatter.title: Traditional
frontmatter.description: Classic Greek cooking with all ingredients

children.content:
# Traditional Greek Cooking

Full range of traditional Greek cuisine without dietary restrictions.

## Includes
- All meats (lamb, pork, chicken, beef)
- Dairy products (feta, yogurt, cheese)
- Eggs
- Seafood
- All vegetables and legumes

## Typical Meals
Balanced meals following traditional patterns with protein, vegetables, and starches served family-style.

## Cooking Tips
- Use generous olive oil
- Don't rush the cooking process
- Serve dishes at proper temperature (many are room temp)

frontmatter.title: Vegetarian (Ladera)
frontmatter.description: Greek vegetarian cooking centered on olive oil dishes

children.content:
# Ladera (Λαδερά) - Vegetarian Greek

Greek cuisine has a rich vegetarian tradition called "ladera" — dishes cooked in olive oil without meat.

## Philosophy
- Vegetables as main course, not side dish
- Olive oil is the star ingredient
- Slow cooking develops deep flavors
- Religious fasting created these traditions

## Core Dishes
- Fasolakia (green beans in tomato)
- Bamies (okra in tomato sauce)
- Briam (mixed roasted vegetables)
- Gigantes plaki (giant beans)
- Gemista (stuffed vegetables - meatless version)
- Spanakopita (spinach pie)

## Cooking Tips
- Don't skimp on olive oil — it's meant to be generous
- Cook until vegetables are very tender
- Serve at room temperature
- Fresh bread is essential

config.label: Dietary Style
config.description: Dietary preferences or restrictions for the cooking

frontmatter.title: Crete
frontmatter.description: Cretan Mediterranean diet and unique ingredients

children.content:
# Crete (Κρήτη)

Cretan cuisine is celebrated worldwide as the foundation of the Mediterranean diet, emphasizing wild greens, olive oil, and simple preparation.

## Key Characteristics
- Exceptionally high olive oil consumption
- Wild greens (horta) as staple
- Less meat, more legumes and vegetables
- Distinctive Cretan cheeses
- Ancient techniques preserved

## Signature Dishes
- Dakos (rusks with tomato)
- Kalitsounia (cheese pies)
- Antikristo (spit-roasted lamb)
- Chochlioi boubouristi (snails)
- Sfakiani pita (cheese pie with honey)

## Pantry Essentials
- Koroneiki olive oil (Cretan extra virgin)
- Rusks (paximadia)
- Mizithra and graviera cheese
- Wild honey
- Dried carob

frontmatter.title: Cyclades Islands
frontmatter.description: Simple, fresh island cooking with seafood focus

children.content:
# Cyclades (Κυκλάδες)

The Cycladic islands (Santorini, Mykonos, Paros, Naxos) feature simple, fresh cuisine shaped by the sea and volcanic soil.

## Key Characteristics
- Fresh seafood emphasis
- Local cheeses (each island unique)
- Volcanic soil ingredients (Santorini)
- Simple preparations highlight quality
- Wind-dried fish and meats

## Signature Dishes
- Fava Santorinis (split pea purée)
- Amorgos pasteli (sesame bars)
- Sun-dried octopus
- Kopanisti (spicy cheese)
- Tomatokeftedes (tomato fritters)

## Pantry Essentials
- Cherry tomatoes (Santorini PDO)
- Capers
- Local cheese varieties
- White eggplant
- Assyrtiko wine (for cooking and drinking)

frontmatter.title: Ionian Islands
frontmatter.description: Western Greek cuisine with Italian influences

children.content:
# Ionian Islands (Επτάνησα)

Corfu, Zakynthos, Kefalonia and the Ionian islands show strong Venetian and Italian influences from centuries of rule.

## Key Characteristics
- Italian-influenced techniques
- Garlic used more liberally
- Wine-based sauces
- Unique spice combinations
- Less Ottoman, more Western European influence

## Signature Dishes
- Sofrito (veal in garlic-wine sauce)
- Pastitsada (spiced meat stew with pasta)
- Bourdeto (spicy fish stew)
- Mandolato (nougat)
- Bianco (white fish soup)

## Pantry Essentials
- White wine
- Nutmeg and cloves
- Fresh parsley (more than other regions)
- Local olive oils
- Graviera cheese

frontmatter.title: Mainland Greece
frontmatter.description: General mainland Greek cooking traditions

children.content:
# Mainland Greece

Traditional cooking from the Greek mainland, combining influences from various regions.

## Key Characteristics
- Generous use of olive oil
- Fresh herbs: oregano, thyme, bay leaves
- Lemon as essential flavoring
- Slow-cooked meat and vegetable dishes

## Signature Dishes
- Moussaka
- Pastitsio
- Lamb kleftiko
- Fassolada (bean soup)
- Kokoretsi

## Pantry Essentials
- Extra virgin olive oil (Peloponnese or Kalamata)
- Dried oregano (rigani)
- Feta cheese (PDO)
- Kalamata olives
- Honey (thyme or pine)

frontmatter.title: Northern Greece
frontmatter.description: Thessaloniki and Macedonia's Ottoman-influenced cuisine

children.content:
# Northern Greece (Βόρεια Ελλάδα)

Thessaloniki and the wider Macedonia region feature robust, hearty cuisine with distinct Ottoman and Balkan influences.

## Key Characteristics
- Richer, spicier flavors
- More use of red pepper and paprika
- Substantial meat dishes
- Ottoman pastry influences
- Dairy and cheese emphasis

## Signature Dishes
- Bougatsa (cream-filled phyllo)
- Soutzoukakia (spiced meatballs)
- Gyros (originated here)
- Koulouri Thessalonikis
- Kavourmas (cured meat)

## Pantry Essentials
- Red pepper flakes (bukovo)
- Kasseri cheese
- Phyllo dough
- Smoked meats
- Trahanas (fermented grain)

config.label: Region
config.description: The Greek region whose culinary traditions to follow

config.label: Greek Cuisine
config.description: Regional styles, culinary traditions, and dietary preferences in Greek cooking

frontmatter.title: Chicken
frontmatter.description: Versatile poultry for everyday Greek meals

children.content:
# Chicken (Κοτόπουλο)

Chicken is a staple protein in Greek cooking, prepared simply with robust Mediterranean flavors.

## Best Cuts
- **Whole bird**: Roasting, traditional
- **Thighs (Μπουτάκια)**: Braising, grilling
- **Breast (Στήθος)**: Souvlaki, quick dishes
- **Wings (Φτερούγες)**: Lemon-roasted

## Classic Preparations
- Kotopoulo lemonato (lemon chicken with potatoes)
- Chicken stifado (with pearl onions)
- Kotopoulo kokkinisto (in tomato sauce)
- Chicken souvlaki
- Kotosoupa avgolemono (chicken soup)

## Cooking Tips
- Dark meat holds up better to Greek slow-cooking
- Marinate with yogurt for tenderness
- Chicken with lemon and oregano is foolproof
- Skin-on for roasting, skinless for stews

## Avgolemono Soup
The classic Greek comfort dish:
- Whole chicken simmered with rice
- Finished with egg-lemon sauce
- Perfect for cold weather and recovery

frontmatter.title: Fish
frontmatter.description: Fresh Mediterranean fish for Greek seafood dishes

children.content:
# Fish (Ψάρι)

Fresh fish is the pride of coastal Greek cuisine, prepared simply to showcase quality.

## Common Greek Fish
- **Lavraki (Sea Bass)**: Most prized, grilled whole
- **Tsipoura (Sea Bream)**: Sweet, delicate flesh
- **Barbounia (Red Mullet)**: Small, pan-fried whole
- **Bakaliaros (Cod)**: Salted/fresh, fried with skordalia
- **Sardeles (Sardines)**: Grilled, fried, or marinated
- **Marides (Whitebait)**: Dusted and fried crispy

## Cooking Methods
- **Grilling**: Whole fish, olive oil, lemon
- **Plaki**: Baked with tomatoes and vegetables
- **Fried**: Dusted in flour, fried crispy
- **Marinated**: Raw or cured with lemon

## Cooking Tips
- Fresh fish should smell like the sea, not fishy
- Score flesh for even cooking
- Don't flip fish more than once
- Let rest 2-3 minutes before serving
- Always have lemon wedges available

## The Greek Way
Whole fish, simply grilled, is considered the finest meal. Don't complicate perfection.

frontmatter.title: Lamb
frontmatter.description: The quintessential Greek protein

children.content:
# Lamb (Αρνί)

Lamb is the most celebrated meat in Greek cuisine, central to holidays and special occasions.

## Best Cuts
- **Leg (Μπούτι)**: Roasting, kleftiko
- **Shoulder (Σπάλα)**: Braising, slow-cooking
- **Chops (Παϊδάκια)**: Grilling
- **Ground (Κιμάς)**: Moussaka, keftedes

## Classic Preparations
- Arni kleftiko (parchment-roasted)
- Arni yiouvetsi (with orzo)
- Lamb souvlaki
- Whole roasted lamb (Easter)
- Arnaki fricassee (with lettuce and dill)

## Cooking Tips
- Bring to room temperature before cooking
- Greek lamb is smaller than American — adjust times
- Pair with lemon, oregano, and garlic
- Don't skip the fat — it's flavorful
- Rest well before cutting

## Sourcing
- Young lamb preferred (milk-fed best)
- Look for Greek or Mediterranean lamb if possible
- Spring lamb is most prized

frontmatter.title: Legumes
frontmatter.description: Beans, lentils, and chickpeas for hearty Greek dishes

children.content:
# Legumes (Όσπρια)

Legumes are the backbone of Greek home cooking — nutritious, economical, and delicious.

## Essential Legumes
- **Fasolia (White Beans)**: Fasolada, gigantes
- **Fakes (Lentils)**: Lentil soup
- **Revithia (Chickpeas)**: Stews, salads
- **Koukia (Fava Beans)**: Fresh or dried
- **Fava (Split Peas)**: Santorini specialty

## Classic Preparations
- Fasolada — the national dish (bean soup)
- Gigantes plaki — giant beans in tomato
- Fakes — lentil soup with vinegar
- Fava — Santorini split pea purée
- Revithada — chickpea stew (Sifnos)

## Cooking Tips
- Soak dried beans overnight (except lentils)
- Add salt after cooking (prevents tough skins)
- Dried beans need 1-2 hours cooking
- Finish with olive oil and lemon
- Better the next day

## Fasting Food
Legumes are central to Greek Orthodox fasting — satisfying, protein-rich, and meat-free.

frontmatter.title: Pork
frontmatter.description: Everyday protein in Greek cooking

children.content:
# Pork (Χοιρινό)

Pork is the most common everyday meat in Greek households, versatile and economical.

## Best Cuts
- **Tenderloin (Φιλέτο)**: Quick cooking, souvlaki
- **Shoulder (Σπάλα)**: Braising, slow cooking
- **Chops (Μπριζόλες)**: Grilling, pan-frying
- **Belly (Λαρδί)**: Crispy roast pork

## Classic Preparations
- Tigania (pan-fried with wine and peppers)
- Hirino me selino (pork with celery avgolemono)
- Pork souvlaki
- Christmas roast pork
- Cured pork products (syglino, apaki)

## Cooking Tips
- Don't overcook — modern pork can be slightly pink
- Wine deglazing is essential for pan dishes
- Complement with dried herbs and citrus
- Fat renders well for crispy results

## Regional Notes
- Apaki (smoked pork) from Crete
- Syglino from Mani peninsula
- Traditionally cured for winter months

frontmatter.title: Vegetables
frontmatter.description: Plant-based main ingredients for Greek dishes

children.content:
# Vegetables (Λαχανικά)

In Greek cuisine, vegetables are often the main event, not just accompaniments.

## Star Vegetables
- **Eggplant (Μελιτζάνα)**: Moussaka, melitzanosalata, imam baildi
- **Tomatoes (Ντομάτες)**: Base of many sauces, stuffed
- **Zucchini (Κολοκύθια)**: Fritters, stuffed, in pies
- **Green Beans (Φασολάκια)**: Ladera dishes
- **Peppers (Πιπεριές)**: Stuffed, roasted, fried

## Ladera Philosophy
Vegetables slow-cooked in generous olive oil until meltingly tender. Not stir-fried or crisp-tender.

## Classic Preparations
- Gemista (stuffed tomatoes and peppers)
- Briam (roasted vegetable medley)
- Imam baildi (stuffed eggplant)
- Fasolakia (green beans in tomato)
- Bamies (okra in tomato sauce)
- Kolokithokeftedes (zucchini fritters)

## Wild Greens (Horta)
Essential to Greek diet:
- Foraged dandelion, chicory, wild spinach
- Boiled, dressed with olive oil and lemon
- Serve at room temperature

config.label: Main Ingredient
config.description: Primary protein or base ingredient for the dish

frontmatter.title: Extra Virgin Olive Oil
frontmatter.description: Premium Greek olive oil for finishing and light cooking

children.content:
# Extra Virgin Olive Oil (Εξαιρετικό Παρθένο)

The gold standard for Greek cooking — use generously and proudly.

## Characteristics
- First cold pressing
- Acidity below 0.8%
- Distinct fruity/peppery notes
- Green to golden color

## Greek Varieties
- **Koroneiki** (Peloponnese): Most common, balanced
- **Kolovi** (Lesbos): Gentle, fruity
- **Adramytini** (Northern Greece): Aromatic
- **Tsounati** (Crete): Robust, peppery

## Best Uses
- Finishing dishes (drizzle to serve)
- Salad dressings
- Dips (tzatziki, fava)
- Low-heat sautéing
- Bread dipping

## Quality Indicators
- Harvest date on label (fresher = better)
- Dark bottle (protects from light)
- Peppery finish (polyphenols)
- Single origin preferred

## Greek Wisdom
"Λάδι στο λάδι" — oil upon oil. In Greek cooking, there's no such thing as too much good olive oil.

frontmatter.title: Pure/Light Olive Oil
frontmatter.description: Refined olive oil for high-heat cooking and frying

children.content:
# Pure/Light Olive Oil

Refined olive oil suitable for high-heat cooking where extra virgin would burn.

## Characteristics
- Blend of refined and virgin olive oil
- Higher smoke point (~465°F / 240°C)
- Neutral flavor
- Lighter color

## Best Uses
- Deep frying
- High-heat sautéing  
- Baking (when neutral flavor needed)
- General cooking base

## Greek Frying Tradition
Greeks traditionally fry in olive oil (not vegetable oil):
- Fried fish and seafood
- Keftedes (meatballs)
- Fried potatoes
- Zucchini fritters

## Tips
- Can be reused 2-3 times if strained
- Still healthier than seed oils
- Combine with butter for flavor
- Finish fried dishes with extra virgin drizzle

## Note
Many Greeks use extra virgin for everything, including frying. The oil degrades but remains usable. Budget accordingly.

config.label: Olive Oil Quality
config.description: Grade of olive oil to use in cooking

frontmatter.title: Autumn
frontmatter.description: Grape harvest, wild mushrooms, and transition ingredients

children.content:
# Autumn (Φθινόπωρο)

Harvest season brings grapes, mushrooms, and the transition to heartier dishes.

## Peak Ingredients
- Grapes (wine-making season)
- Wild mushrooms
- Quinces (kydonia)
- Pomegranates
- Chestnuts
- Late figs
- Squash and pumpkin
- Fresh olive oil (new harvest!)

## Seasonal Dishes
- Stifado (beef or rabbit with onions)
- Quince spoon sweet
- Mushroom dishes
- Pumpkin pie (kolokythopita)
- Trahanas soup
- Chestnut desserts

## New Olive Oil (Agourelaio)
The first pressing of the season:
- Vibrant green color
- Peppery, intense flavor
- Best drizzled fresh
- Greeks celebrate this moment

## Cooking Transition
- Shift to oven-braised dishes
- Start slow-cooking stews
- Preserve summer's bounty
- Prepare pantry for winter

frontmatter.title: Spring
frontmatter.description: Fresh greens, Easter lamb, and rebirth ingredients

children.content:
# Spring (Άνοιξη)

Spring brings tender new ingredients and the most important Greek holiday — Easter.

## Peak Ingredients
- Young lamb (milk-fed)
- Artichokes (aginares)
- Fresh broad beans (koukia)
- Wild greens at their best
- Fresh dill and fennel
- Spring onions
- Green almonds
- Fresh peas

## Seasonal Dishes
- Artichokes à la polita
- Lamb fricassee with lettuce
- Fresh fava beans with dill
- Magiritsa (Easter soup)
- Kokoretsi (Easter offal)
- Tsoureki (Easter bread)

## Easter (Πάσχα)
The biggest Greek celebration:
- Lamb is mandatory
- Red eggs (kokkina avga)
- Magiritsa on Saturday midnight
- Whole lamb on Sunday
- Kourabiedes and tsoureki

## Cooking Notes
- Vegetables are tender — shorter cooking times
- Fresh artichokes need lemon water to prevent browning
- Take advantage of wild horta abundance

frontmatter.title: Summer
frontmatter.description: Fresh tomatoes, seafood, and outdoor grilling season

children.content:
# Summer (Καλοκαίρι)

Greek summer means ripe tomatoes, fresh seafood, and long outdoor meals.

## Peak Ingredients
- Tomatoes at their best
- Eggplant (melitzana)
- Zucchini and its flowers
- Fresh figs
- Watermelon
- Fresh fish and seafood
- Peppers of all varieties
- Cucumbers
- Peaches and apricots

## Seasonal Dishes
- Greek salad (horiatiki) — peak season
- Gemista (stuffed vegetables)
- Briam (summer vegetable roast)
- Grilled fresh fish
- Melitzanosalata
- Kolokithokeftedes (zucchini fritters)
- Spoon sweets from summer fruit

## Cooking Style
- Lighter preparations
- Room temperature dishes (ladera)
- Grilling outdoors
- Fresh over cooked
- Stay hydrated!

## Entertaining
Summer evenings are made for:
- Meze on the veranda
- Ouzo and tsipouro
- Grilled seafood
- Fresh watermelon to finish

frontmatter.title: Winter
frontmatter.description: Hearty stews, citrus, and Christmas celebrations

children.content:
# Winter (Χειμώνας)

Greek winter cooking is all about warmth, comfort, and holiday celebrations.

## Peak Ingredients
- Citrus (oranges, lemons, mandarins)
- Cabbage and leafy greens
- Root vegetables
- Dried legumes
- Preserved meats
- Fresh pork (slaughter season)
- Cauliflower
- Celery root

## Seasonal Dishes
- Avgolemono soup
- Fasolada (bean soup)
- Pork with celery (hirino me selino)
- Lahano dolmades (cabbage rolls)
- Portokalopita (orange cake)
- Karydopita (walnut cake)
- Christmas cookies (melomakarona, kourabiedes)

## Holiday Cooking
**Christmas (Χριστούγεννα)**
- Roast pork or turkey
- Christopsomo (Christmas bread)
- Melomakarona and kourabiedes mandatory

**New Year (Πρωτοχρονιά)**
- Vasilopita (coin cake)
- Pork continues
- Good luck foods

## Cooking Style
- Long braises and stews
- Oven dishes
- Warm soups
- Hearty legumes
- Preserved ingredients shine

config.label: Ingredients
config.description: Core Greek ingredients, seasonal availability, and substitutions

config.label: Occasion
config.description: Type of event or gathering for the meal

config.label: Meal Planning
config.description: Meal types, occasions, courses, and serving configurations

frontmatter.title: Stovetop Braised
frontmatter.description: Slow-cooked dishes in sauce (yiahni style)

children.content:
# Stovetop Braised (Κατσαρόλα / Γιαχνί)

Slow-simmered dishes in sauce — the heart of Greek home cooking.

## Technique Basics
- Start with softened aromatics
- Build flavor in layers
- Low heat, long time
- Don't rush the process

## Yiahni Method
The foundational technique:
1. Sweat onions in olive oil until soft
2. Add garlic briefly
3. Add tomato paste, stir 1 minute
4. Add tomatoes and liquid
5. Add meat or vegetables
6. Simmer until very tender

## Classic Braised Dishes
- Keftedes (meatballs in sauce)
- Soutzoukakia (spiced meatballs)
- Beef stifado (with pearl onions)
- Fasolakia yiahni (green beans)
- Octopus stifado
- Moschari kokkinisto (beef in tomato)

## Tips
- Taste and adjust seasoning throughout
- Add water if reducing too fast
- Cinnamon and allspice are common
- Finish with fresh parsley

## Timing
Most braised dishes need 1-2 hours minimum. Better the next day.

frontmatter.title: Fried
frontmatter.description: Pan-frying and deep-frying Greek specialties

children.content:
# Fried (Τηγανητά)

Greeks love fried food — from fish to cheese to vegetables.

## Technique Basics
- Use olive oil (traditional)
- Moderate temperature (not smoking)
- Don't overcrowd the pan
- Drain on paper towels
- Serve immediately

## Pan-Fried Dishes
- Small fish (marides, barbounia)
- Saganaki (fried cheese)
- Keftedakia (mini meatballs)
- Tigania (pork pan-fried with wine)
- Eggs (Greek-style with olive oil)

## Deep-Fried Dishes
- Kalamari (squid rings)
- Bakaliaros (cod)
- Kolokithokeftedes (zucchini fritters)
- Tyropitakia (cheese triangles)
- Loukoumades (honey puffs)

## Flour Coating
For fish and vegetables:
- Light flour dusting
- No batter typically
- Seasoned flour (salt, pepper)
- Shake off excess

## The Greek Touch
- Serve with lemon wedges always
- Skordalia (garlic dip) for fish
- Tzatziki for fritters
- Hot from the pan to plate

frontmatter.title: Grilled
frontmatter.description: Open flame cooking for meats and seafood

children.content:
# Grilled (Στα Κάρβουνα)

Charcoal grilling is beloved in Greece — for souvlaki, fish, and more.

## Technique Basics
- Charcoal preferred over gas
- High heat for souvlaki, moderate for fish
- Oil the food, not the grill
- Let rest before serving

## Classic Grilled Items
- Souvlaki (pork, chicken, lamb)
- Whole fish (lavraki, tsipoura)
- Lamb chops (paidakia)
- Biftekia (Greek burgers)
- Octopus
- Halloumi cheese
- Vegetables (peppers, eggplant)

## Greek Grilling Style
- Marinate with olive oil, lemon, oregano
- Don't over-flip — once is enough
- Squeeze lemon at the table
- Serve immediately

## Souvlaki Specifics
- Cut meat in 1-inch cubes
- Alternate with onion/pepper optional
- Grill hot and fast
- Rest briefly, serve in pita

## Equipment
- Charcoal grill (foukou)
- Metal skewers (souvles)
- Grill basket for fish
- Wire brush for cleaning

frontmatter.title: Ladera (Olive Oil Stewed)
frontmatter.description: Vegetable dishes cooked in generous olive oil

children.content:
# Ladera (Λαδερά)

Unique Greek technique: vegetables slow-cooked in olive oil until meltingly tender.

## Philosophy
- Olive oil is a primary ingredient, not a cooking fat
- Vegetables cook until very soft (not crisp-tender)
- Served at room temperature
- Perfect for fasting periods
- Improves overnight

## Technique
1. Sauté aromatics in generous olive oil
2. Add vegetables
3. Add tomatoes (usually)
4. Add water to almost cover
5. Simmer uncovered until oil rises
6. Cool to room temperature

## Classic Ladera Dishes
- Fasolakia (green beans)
- Bamies (okra)
- Arakas (peas)
- Gigantes plaki (giant beans)
- Imam baildi (stuffed eggplant)
- Briam (mixed vegetables)
- Aginares ala polita (artichokes)

## How Much Oil?
More than you think. Traditionally, the oil should "swim" on top when done. At least 1/2 cup for a pot.

## Serving
- Room temperature (not cold, not hot)
- Fresh bread for sopping
- Feta cheese on the side
- Squeeze of lemon optional

frontmatter.title: Oven-Roasted
frontmatter.description: Traditional Greek oven cooking (sto fourno)

children.content:
# Oven-Roasted (Στο Φούρνο)

The most common method in Greek home cooking — set it and forget it.

## Technique Basics
- Low and slow is typical (150-180°C / 300-350°F)
- Generous olive oil base
- Lemon and oregano finish
- Cover early, uncover to brown

## Classic Oven Dishes
- Whole roast lamb or chicken with potatoes
- Moussaka and pastitsio
- Yemista (stuffed vegetables)
- Yiouvetsi (meat with orzo)
- Briam (vegetable medley)
- Fish plaki

## Equipment
- Heavy roasting pan (tapsi)
- Clay pots for traditional cooking
- Parchment for kleftiko-style

## Greek Oven Wisdom
- Potatoes absorb meat juices beautifully
- Turn items midway for even cooking
- Baste with pan juices
- Rest meat before cutting
- Everything is better next day

## Temperature Guide
- Quick roast: 200°C / 400°F (40-60 min)
- Standard: 180°C / 350°F (1-2 hours)
- Low and slow: 150°C / 300°F (3+ hours)

config.label: Cooking Method
config.description: Primary technique for preparing the dish

frontmatter.title: Outdoor Grill
frontmatter.description: Charcoal or gas grill for Greek-style cooking

children.content:
# Outdoor Grill (Ψησταριά)

Essential for authentic Greek grilled dishes — preferably charcoal.

## Ideal Setup
- Charcoal grill (preferred)
- Gas grill (acceptable)
- Multiple heat zones
- Lid for indirect cooking

## Greek Grilling Equipment
- Metal souvlaki skewers (flat, not round)
- Long tongs
- Fish grilling basket
- Basting brush
- Heat-resistant gloves

## Best For
- Souvlaki of all types
- Paidakia (lamb chops)
- Whole fish
- Biftekia
- Grilled halloumi
- Grilled vegetables
- Octopus

## Charcoal Tips
- Wait for white ash coating
- Create hot and cool zones
- Don't flip too often
- Rest meat off the grill

## Greek Tradition
Sunday grilling is a social event. The grill master (usually male, traditionally) tends the fire while others prepare sides and enjoy drinks.

frontmatter.title: Standard Kitchen
frontmatter.description: Basic home kitchen equipment

children.content:
# Standard Kitchen

Essential equipment found in any home kitchen — sufficient for most Greek cooking.

## Basic Equipment
- Chef's knife and paring knife
- Cutting boards
- Large stock pot
- Medium saucepan
- Large skillet/frying pan
- Baking sheets
- 9x13 baking dish
- Mixing bowls
- Colander

## Greek Essentials
- Heavy roasting pan (tapsi)
- Large deep skillet with lid
- Wooden spoons
- Kitchen towels (many!)
- Lemon squeezer

## What You Can Make
- All soups and stews
- Ladera dishes
- Simple roasts
- Pan-fried dishes
- Salads and dips
- Most baked dishes

## Nice to Have
- Food processor (for dips)
- Immersion blender
- Kitchen scale
- Instant-read thermometer

## Greek Kitchen Philosophy
Greek cooking doesn't require fancy equipment. Simple tools, quality ingredients, and patience are the keys.

frontmatter.title: Traditional Equipment
frontmatter.description: Authentic Greek cooking vessels and tools

children.content:
# Traditional Equipment

Specialty items for authentic Greek cooking experiences.

## Clay Cookware
- **Giouvetsi pot**: Clay pot for oven-baked dishes with orzo
- **Briki**: Small pot for Greek coffee
- **Saganaki pan**: Two-handled small frying pan

## Phyllo Tools
- Rolling pin (long, thin style)
- Pastry brush (for butter)
- Clean tablecloth (for stretching)

## Specialty Items
- Mortar and pestle (for skordalia)
- Cheese grater (for graviera, kefalotyri)
- Mandoline (for even vegetable slices)
- Meat grinder or attachment

## Traditional Baking
- Circular baking pans (for pies)
- Tapsi (large rectangular roasting pan)
- Clay bread dome (for village bread)

## Where to Find
- Greek specialty stores
- Mediterranean markets
- Online retailers
- Bring back from Greece!

## Worth the Investment
- Quality clay pots last generations
- Traditional briki makes better coffee
- Heavy tapsi distributes heat evenly
- Good mortar produces better texture

frontmatter.title: Advanced
frontmatter.description: Complex techniques for experienced cooks

children.content:
# Advanced Level (Προχωρημένος)

For cooks ready to tackle complex Greek specialties.

## Characteristics
- Multi-day preparation
- Precise technique required
- Multiple advanced skills combined
- High stakes (expensive ingredients)
- Traditional mastery

## Advanced Dishes
- Handmade phyllo dough
- Galaktoboureko (custard in phyllo)
- Whole roasted lamb
- Kokoretsi (Easter offal)
- Handmade pasta (hilopites)
- Complex baklava
- Preserved foods (spoon sweets)
- Magiritsa (Easter soup)

## Skills Required
- Phyllo rolling and stretching
- Custard making
- Syrup temperatures
- Whole animal butchery
- Preservation techniques
- Perfect timing on proteins
- Multi-course orchestration

## Challenges
- Phyllo tears easily
- Custard can curdle
- Syrup crystallization
- Offal preparation
- Holiday meal timing

## The Yiayia Standard
These are dishes that take years to perfect. Expect to make them many times before mastery. Even grandmothers continue improving.

frontmatter.title: Beginner
frontmatter.description: Simple recipes for new cooks

children.content:
# Beginner Level (Αρχάριος)

Perfect Greek dishes for those just starting their cooking journey.

## Characteristics
- Few ingredients
- Forgiving techniques
- 30 minutes or less active time
- Minimal knife work
- Hard to mess up

## Beginner-Friendly Dishes
- Greek salad (no cooking!)
- Tzatziki (mixing only)
- Horta (boiled greens with olive oil)
- Fakes (lentil soup)
- Greek omelette
- Grilled cheese (saganaki)
- Simple grilled chicken

## Skills to Learn
- Properly dressing a salad
- Salting eggplant
- Boiling and draining
- Basic knife cuts
- Timing pasta/rice

## Tips for Success
- Read recipe fully before starting
- Prep all ingredients first (mise en place)
- Taste as you go
- Use a timer
- Don't be afraid of olive oil

## Equipment Needed
- Good chef's knife
- Cutting board
- Large pot
- Skillet/frying pan
- Colander

frontmatter.title: Intermediate
frontmatter.description: Most traditional Greek recipes

children.content:
# Intermediate Level (Μέτριος)

The sweet spot for most authentic Greek home cooking.

## Characteristics
- Multiple components
- 1-2 hours total time
- Layered techniques
- Some timing coordination
- Building flavor profiles

## Intermediate Dishes
- Moussaka (layered casserole)
- Pastitsio (pasta bake)
- Gemista (stuffed vegetables)
- Lamb kleftiko
- Keftedes (meatballs)
- Spanakopita (using store phyllo)
- Most meat stews

## Skills Required
- Béchamel sauce
- Proper meat browning
- Sauce making
- Multiple burner management
- Vegetable prep techniques
- Basic sauce emulsion (avgolemono)

## Challenges to Master
- Timing multiple dishes
- Not overcooking proteins
- Seasoning in layers
- Proper resting
- Temperature control

## Growth Areas
Once comfortable, try:
- Homemade phyllo
- Complex layered dishes
- Whole roasted meats
- Multi-course meals

config.label: Skill Level
config.description: Cooking experience required for the recipe

config.label: Cooking Techniques
config.description: Traditional Greek cooking methods, equipment, and skill levels
